name: The Borrow Smith
personality: A patient artisan who speaks softly but precisely. Every sentence has a lifetime. She can tell you exactly when a reference goes out of scope. Wears heat-resistant gloves stained orange from the borrow-checker fires.
greeting: Welcome to the forge. The fires are hot — that's the compiler checking your work in real time. Start with the ownership tile by the door.

dialog_tree.response: Every value has exactly one owner. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped. No exceptions. No garbage collector needed. Tile #3001 will teach you the rhythm.

People curse it. I love it. The borrow checker is the forge — it burns away bugs before they're born. You can have many readers OR one writer, never both at once. This isn't a limitation, it's a guarantee.

Ah, my favorite. Compile-time parameters. You say struct Kernel<const N: usize> and the compiler generates a specialized version for every N. Stack-allocated, zero overhead. Tile #3002 shows the patterns.
Purity. No standard library, no heap, no compromise. A no_std kernel compiles everywhere — microcontrollers, WASM, your toaster. Tile #3003 is the path to bare metal enlightenment.
The chamber next door? Respectable. Fortran beats us on autovectorization — for now. But our safety guarantees are worth the 20% throughput gap. And explicit SIMD closes it further.
Lanes of parallel fire. Fortran gets this for free from gfortran. We have to be explicit — packed_simd, f64x4, f64x8. But explicit means we control every lane. Tile #3004 if you want to go deep.
The anvil is ready. For a no_std snap kernel, bring me Tiles #3001 and #3003. For SIMD batch processing, bring #3002 and #3004. I'll forge what you need.
Yes, it's slow. 12 seconds for a clean build. But every second of compile time is a bug that won't happen at runtime. What's your production outage cost? Exactly. The forge is cheap insurance.
